the Harmonized System of Survey and Certification (HSSC), 2025 adopted by the 34
th

session of the Assembly of the International Maritime Organization (IMO).

香 港 商 船 資 訊

HONG KONG MERCHANT SHIPPING INFORMATION NOTE
Survey Guidelines under the Harmonized System of Survey and
Certification (HSSC), 2025

To : Shipowners, Ship Managers, Ship Operators, Ship Masters and Classification Societies

Summary

The purpose of this Note is to advise the parties concerned of the Survey Guidelines under
the Harmonized System of Survey and Certification (HSSC), 2025 adopted by the 34
th

session of the Assembly of the International Maritime Organization (IMO).

1. The 34
th
session of the Assembly adopted on 3 December 2025 the Survey Guidelines
under the Harmonized System of Survey and Certification (HSSC), 2025 (Survey Guidelines) as
set out in the annex to resolution A.1207(34).

2. The Survey Guidelines is adopted to facilitate the harmonized, consistent and timely
global implementation of certain IMO mandatory instruments and provide guidance to the work
in relation to the survey and certification.

3. The details of the Survey Guidelines as set out in the annex to resolution A.1207(34)
can be found on the website of Marine Department
(https://www.mardep.gov.hk/en/legislation/notices/msin/index.html) as attachment to this Note.

4. Shipowners, Ship Managers, Ship Operators, Masters and Classification Societies are
advised to note the Survey Guidelines and act accordingly.

Marine Department
Multi-lateral Policy Division

12 February 2026

notices.title: A collision accident happened in the river waterways
notices.subject: A collision accident happened in the river waterways
notices.summary:
A Hong Kong-registered bulk carrier (the bulk carrier) and a Panama-registered container
vessel (the container vessel) were involved in a collision in the river waterways. While both
vessels were navigating within the river channel, the bow of the outbound container vessel
crashed into the aft port hull of the inbound bulk carrier, causing serious hull damage to both
vessels. The collision also resulted in fuel oil leakage, leading to pollution of the local
environment. This Note draws the attention of shipowners, ship managers, ship operators,
masters, officers and crew to the lessons learnt from this accident.

pages.text:
Marine Department
Harbour Building
38 Pier Road
G.P.O. Box 4155
Hong Kong
Telephone No. : (852) 2852 3001
Fax No. : (852) 2544 9241
E-mail : hkmpd@mardep.gov.hk
Web site : https://www.mardep.gov.hk
No. : 2/2026

香港商船資訊

HONG KONG MERCHANT SHIPPING INFORMATION NOTE
A collision accident happened in the river waterways
To : Shipowners, Ship Managers, Ship Operators, Masters, Officers and Crew
Summary
A Hong Kong-registered bulk carrier (the bulk carrier) and a Panama-registered container
vessel (the container vessel) were involved in a collision in the river waterways. While both
vessels were navigating within the river channel, the bow of the outbound container vessel
crashed into the aft port hull of the inbound bulk carrier, causing serious hull damage to both
vessels. The collision also resulted in fuel oil leakage, leading to pollution of the local
environment. This Note draws the attention of shipowners, ship managers, ship operators,
masters, officers and crew to the lessons learnt from this accident.
The Incident
1. On the day of the incident, the container vessel completed its cargo operations and
departed from the river port under pilotage. Meanwhile, the bulk carrier picked up the local
pilot and entered the river channel en route to the loading port to take on more cargo. About
five minutes before the collision, the pilots of both vessels communicated via VHF
radio-telephone in their local language to coordinate safe passage through a curved channel,
which was about 150 metres wide. Subsequently, the bulk carrier altered course to starboard
under a hard-starboard rudder, while the container vessel altered course to port under the pilot’s
orders. Eventually, the collision occurred as both vessels were adjusting their headings within
the curved channel. The bow of the container vessel struck the port hull of the bulk carrier,
rupturing two fuel oil tanks and two cargo holds, which exposed the interior structure. Fuel oil
leaked from the ruptured tanks into the river, and the damaged cargo holds were flooded with
contaminated river water. The bow structure of the container vessel was also damaged, either
torn or deformed. After the collision, the two vessels became entangled and drifted with the
current into shallow waters, where they eventually ran aground.

2. The investigation identified that the contributory factors leading to the incident were
as follows:
(i) both vessels were expected to pass each other at the turn of the curved channel,
but they failed to reach a prior agreement on an appropriate position to avoid
collision, such as coordinating a speed reduction to avoid meeting at the
curved section of the channel. This failure created difficulties in taking
avoiding action and posed safety risks, especially for large vessels;
(ii) the bulk carrier altered course to starboard along the curve cape, but the
chosen turning position did not adequately account for factors such as the
vessel’s speed, maneuvering characteristics, and the effect of the
following-current which pushed the bulk carrier to the channel center and
encroached on the container vessel’s lane with an imminent risk of collision.
In addition, the pilot and the Master of the bulk carrier did not fully
understand the maneuvering characteristics of the vessel, and the information
that “reducing speed will take 10 minutes”
1
affected their ability to make
timely decision on maneuvering the vessel’s speed;
(iii) both vessels were not navigating at an appropriate safe speed to ensure
effective collision avoidance;
(iv) the container vessel initiated to maneuver to port side too early when the bulk
carrier was turning to starboard, causing the bulk carrier to drift and encroach
on the container vessel’s lane under the influence of the following-current,
thereby creating the risk of collision. Furthermore, at the critical moment,
the helmsman of the container vessel did not correctly follow the pilot’s order
on collision avoidance;
(v) neither vessel effectively utilising available equipment to acquire, monitor,
and continuously track the opposite’s movements, nor did they maintain
proper lookout and effective watchkeeping;
(vi) both vessels failed to adequately assess the collision risk, and the
collision-avoidance measures taken were inappropriate;
(vii) there was a lack of coordination between the pilots and the bridge teams.
The pilots’ maneuvering actions on the bridge were not verified and
monitored properly, leaving the pilots to make independent decisions on
collision avoidance;

1
After boarding the bulk carrier, the Master and the pilot exchanged information regarding the vessel's parameters
and maneuvering characteristics, including the engine response time, where the pilot was informed that “reducing
speed would take 10 minutes”.

(viii) the pilots failed to convey relevant information regarding their navigation
communication in English in a timely manner, which would have effectively
supported and monitored the pilots and enabled effective and timely responses
to the pilots’ actions; and
(ix) the pilot of the bulk carrier continuously chatted on a mobile phone for
personal matters during the vessel’s maneuvering, which distracted the bridge
team and hindered effective coordination on the bridge.
Lessons Learnt
3. In order to avoid recurrence of similar accidents in the future, ship management
companies, all masters, officers and crew members should note:
(a) vessels navigating in a fairway, including those under pilotage, should agree in
advance with the approaching vessels on a location to pass safely, especially in
areas with limited or obscured visibility, strong currents, or curved narrow
fairway;
(b) vessels should maintain proper lookout and adjust speed as appropriate to
avoid meeting at the curved area of narrow waters to minimise the risk of
collision;
(c) masters and pilots should be fully aware of the vessel’s maneuvering
characteristics, as well as local current and tidal conditions, and should
properly maneuver the vessel under the prevailing hydrographic conditions;
(d) vessels should strictly adhere to Rule 9 of the International Regulations for
Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972 when navigating in narrow channels;
(e) ensure pilot’s orders on maneuvering are carefully verified and correctly
executed;
(f) ensure effective communication between pilot and bridge navigation team
whenever safe navigation is involved; and
(g) masters and deck officers should closely monitor the pilot’s conduct on the
bridge, including any use of personal electronic devices which might affect
safe navigation, and should immediately take over the navigation if safe
navigation is endangered.

4. The attention of shipowners, ship managers, ship operators, masters, officers and
crew is drawn to the lessons learnt above.

Marine Department
Multi-lateral Policy Division

14 January 2026

HONG KONG MERCHANT SHIPPING INFORMATION NOTE

To : Shipowners, Ship Managers, Ship Operators, Masters, Officers and Crew

1
affected their ability to make

1
After boarding the bulk carrier, the Master and the pilot exchanged information regarding the vessel's parameters
and maneuvering characteristics, including the engine response time, where the pilot was informed that “reducing
speed would take 10 minutes”.

(viii) the pilots failed to convey relevant information regarding their navigation
communication in English in a timely manner, which would have effectively

(ix) the pilot of the bulk carrier continuously chatted on a mobile phone for

Multi-lateral Policy Division

14 January 2026

encroached on the container vessel’s lane with an imminent risk of collision.
In addition, the pilot and the Master of the bulk carrier did not fully
understand the maneuvering characteristics of the vessel, and the information
that “reducing speed will take 10 minutes”
1
affected their ability to make
timely decision on maneuvering the vessel’s speed;
(iii) both vessels were not navigating at an appropriate safe speed to ensure
effective collision avoidance;
(iv) the container vessel initiated to maneuver to port side too early when the bulk
carrier was turning to starboard, causing the bulk carrier to drift and encroach
on the container vessel’s lane under the influence of the following-current,
thereby creating the risk of collision. Furthermore, at the critical moment,
the helmsman of the container vessel did not correctly follow the pilot’s order
on collision avoidance;
(v) neither vessel effectively utilising available equipment to acquire, monitor,
and continuously track the opposite’s movements, nor did they maintain
proper lookout and effective watchkeeping;
(vi) both vessels failed to adequately assess the collision risk, and the
collision-avoidance measures taken were inappropriate;
(vii) there was a lack of coordination between the pilots and the bridge teams.
The pilots’ maneuvering actions on the bridge were not verified and
monitored properly, leaving the pilots to make independent decisions on
collision avoidance;

1
After boarding the bulk carrier, the Master and the pilot exchanged information regarding the vessel's parameters
and maneuvering characteristics, including the engine response time, where the pilot was informed that “reducing
speed would take 10 minutes”.

(viii) the pilots failed to convey relevant information regarding their navigation
communication in English in a timely manner, which would have effectively
supported and monitored the pilots and enabled effective and timely responses
to the pilots’ actions; and
(ix) the pilot of the bulk carrier continuously chatted on a mobile phone for
personal matters during the vessel’s maneuvering, which distracted the bridge
team and hindered effective coordination on the bridge.
Lessons Learnt
3. In order to avoid recurrence of similar accidents in the future, ship management
companies, all masters, officers and crew members should note:
(a) vessels navigating in a fairway, including those under pilotage, should agree in
advance with the approaching vessels on a location to pass safely, especially in
areas with limited or obscured visibility, strong currents, or curved narrow
fairway;
(b) vessels should maintain proper lookout and adjust speed as appropriate to
avoid meeting at the curved area of narrow waters to minimise the risk of
collision;
(c) masters and pilots should be fully aware of the vessel’s maneuvering
characteristics, as well as local current and tidal conditions, and should
properly maneuver the vessel under the prevailing hydrographic conditions;
(d) vessels should strictly adhere to Rule 9 of the International Regulations for
Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972 when navigating in narrow channels;
(e) ensure pilot’s orders on maneuvering are carefully verified and correctly
executed;
(f) ensure effective communication between pilot and bridge navigation team
whenever safe navigation is involved; and
(g) masters and deck officers should closely monitor the pilot’s conduct on the
bridge, including any use of personal electronic devices which might affect
safe navigation, and should immediately take over the navigation if safe
navigation is endangered.

4. The attention of shipowners, ship managers, ship operators, masters, officers and
crew is drawn to the lessons learnt above.

Marine Department
Multi-lateral Policy Division

14 January 2026

香 港 商 船 資 訊

HONG KONG MERCHANT SHIPPING INFORMATION NOTE
Hong Kong Merchant Shipping Information Notes Current as at
1 January 2026

To : All recipients of Hong Kong Merchant Shipping Information Notes

Summary

This Note provides a list and the subject index of all valid Hong Kong Merchant Shipping
Information Notes (HKMSINs) as at 1 January 2026. This Note supersedes HKMSIN No.
1/2025.

1. An annual exercise has been conducted to review all current HKMSINs with outdated
notes being either revoked or superseded by updated versions. Outcome of the review is at
Annex 1.

2. A list of all valid HKMSINs as at 1 January 2026, respectively sorted by Notice
Number and Subject, is at Annex 2A and Annex 2B.

3. HKMSINs are available the website of the Marine Department
(https://www.mardep.gov.hk/en/legislation/notices/msin/index.html). Enquiries relating to
HKMSINs can be sent by e-mail to: hkmpd@mardep.gov.hk.

4. The HKMSIN No. 1/2025 is hereby cancelled.

Marine Department
Multi-lateral Policy Division

1 January 2026
